past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, moderately controlled
Chronic sinusitis with two episodes of acute bacterial sinusitis in the past year
Mild hearing loss in the right ear following childhood otitis media

medications.dose: 50 mcg per spray, 2 sprays each nostril

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease
Mother alive and well at age 68
No family history of neurological disease or immunodeficiency

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 10 pack-year history, quit 3 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in suburban home
social_history.exercise: Moderate activity level, runs 2-3 times per week
social_history.travel: No recent travel outside the United States

neurological_exam.mental_status: Lethargic but arousable to voice and pain. Oriented to self only; unable to state date, month, or location. Speech is slow with occasional paraphasic errors. Unable to follow two-step commands consistently. Glasgow Coma Scale score 12 (E3 V4 M5).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive to light. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Extraocular movements intact but patient reports photophobia and winces with penlight examination. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly reduced on the right (baseline). Gag reflex intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Moves all four extremities spontaneously but slowly. Strength 4/5 globally, limited by poor effort due to encephalopathy. No focal weakness identified. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain in all extremities symmetrically. Light touch sensation grossly intact but detailed testing limited by encephalopathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally (negative Babinski).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to fully assess due to altered mental status. Finger-to-nose attempted on the right but dysmetric, likely due to inattention rather than cerebellar dysfunction.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate safely due to altered mental status and generalized weakness.
neurological_exam.additional: Marked nuchal rigidity with positive Kernig sign bilaterally (resistance and pain with knee extension when hip is flexed to 90 degrees). Positive Brudzinski sign (involuntary hip and knee flexion with passive neck flexion). Jolt accentuation test markedly positive. No rash or petechiae on full skin examination.

patient.chief_complaint: Severe headache, fever, and confusion over the past 12 hours
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Javier Herrera is a 62-year-old right-handed man with a history of type 2 diabetes, chronic sinusitis, and mild right-sided hearing loss who presents to the emergency department via ambulance with approximately 12 hours of progressively worsening headache, fever, and altered mental status. His wife reports that he came home from work yesterday evening around 5 PM complaining of the worst headache of his life, which was diffuse and throbbing in character. He took ibuprofen 600 mg without relief. Over the ensuing hours, he developed shaking chills and his wife measured a temperature of 102.8°F (39.3°C) at 9 PM. He went to bed early but was restless and moaning throughout the night. By 3 AM, his wife noticed he was confused and not making sense when she tried to talk to him. He was complaining that the bedroom light was too bright and kept his eyes closed. He vomited twice between 3 AM and 5 AM, which was non-bloody and non-bilious. She called 911 at approximately 5:15 AM when she could not fully rouse him and he did not recognize their children. EMS found him febrile at 39.5°C with a heart rate of 122 and blood pressure of 148/82. He was transported emergently. In the ED, he is lethargic, photophobic, and in obvious distress. He is intermittently groaning and reaching for his head. Notably, the patient had been treated for an acute sinusitis exacerbation approximately 10 days ago with a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate, which he completed 3 days prior to this presentation. His wife denies any recent head trauma, seizure activity, rash, ear discharge, or known sick contacts with meningitis. She confirms his immunizations are up to date, though she is uncertain about meningococcal vaccination.

findings.location: Diffuse, bilateral cerebral convexities and along the basal cisterns

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Diffuse smooth leptomeningeal enhancement along bilateral cerebral convexities, sylvian fissures, and basal cisterns
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the sulci bilaterally, consistent with proteinaceous or purulent material
signal_characteristics.DWI: Foci of restricted diffusion within sulci of the bilateral frontoparietal convexities, consistent with purulent material
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal along the sulci bilaterally

findings.mass_effect: Mild generalized cerebral swelling with subtle effacement of cortical sulci. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Diffuse involvement without focal mass lesion

findings.location: Bilateral maxillary sinuses and right ethmoid air cells

signal_characteristics.T2: Mucosal thickening and fluid signal in bilateral maxillary sinuses and right ethmoid air cells
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Mucosal enhancement in the right ethmoid sinus

additional_observations: No subdural empyema or epidural abscess identified
No evidence of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis on MRV sequences
mri.impression: No intracranial complications such as abscess, empyema, or venous sinus thrombosis. Paranasal sinus disease involving bilateral maxillary sinuses and right ethmoid air cells, which may represent a primary source of infection. Mild generalized cerebral edema. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 20.6 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 92 % (H), Band forms 12 % (H), Platelets 142 x10^9/L (L), Sodium 133 mEq/L (L), Chloride 94 mEq/L (L), Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L (L), BUN 25 mg/dL (H), Glucose 191 mg/dL (H), CRP 184 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 8.2 ng/mL (H), ESR 86 mm/hr (H), Lactate 3.8 mmol/L (H), PT 14.7 seconds (H), INR 1.3  (H), Fibrinogen 439 mg/dL (H), D-dimer 2.6 mcg/mL FEU (H), HbA1c 7.6 % (H).

special_tests.Gram_stain: Gram-positive diplococci identified, consistent with Streptococcus pneumoniae
special_tests.CSF_lactate: 12.4 mmol/L (normal <3.5; markedly elevated, highly suggestive of bacterial meningitis)
special_tests.CSF_PCR_meningitis_panel: Streptococcus pneumoniae detected; all other targets negative

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 35 cmH2O. WBC: 3069 cells/microL (normal <5) (94% PMN/4% lymph). Protein: 363.4 mg/dL (normal 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 15.3 mg/dL (normal 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.07 (CSF glucose 14 / serum glucose 198; normal >0.6)). Gram_stain: Gram-positive diplococci identified, consistent with Streptococcus pneumoniae. CSF_lactate: 12.4 mmol/L (normal <3.5; markedly elevated, highly suggestive of bacterial meningitis). Latex_agglutination: Positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen. CSF_PCR_meningitis_panel: Streptococcus pneumoniae detected; all other targets negative.

output.appearance: Slightly hazy but improved from initial LP
output.glucose_ratio: 0.22 (improving but still low; serum glucose 148 mg/dL)

special_tests.CSF_culture: No growth at 48 hours (prior culture grew Streptococcus pneumoniae, penicillin MIC 0.06 mcg/mL - susceptible)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 22 cmH2O. WBC: 690 cells/microL (decreased from 3200) (62% PMN/30% lymph). Protein: 169.3 mg/dL (decreased from 348 mg/dL). Glucose: 34.2 mg/dL (improved from 14 mg/dL) (ratio 0.22 (improving but still low; serum glucose 148 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen. CSF_culture: No growth at 48 hours (prior culture grew Streptococcus pneumoniae, penicillin MIC 0.06 mcg/mL - susceptible). CSF_lactate: 6.2 mmol/L (decreased from 12.4; still elevated).

findings.description: CT: Mild generalized loss of gray-white matter differentiation. Sulcal effacement bilaterally without focal hypodensity to suggest infarction.

findings.location: Bilateral maxillary sinuses and right ethmoid air cells
findings.description: CT: Opacification of bilateral maxillary sinuses and right ethmoid air cells with air-fluid levels. Possible bone erosion along the posterior wall of the right ethmoid sinus requiring further evaluation.

additional_observations: No focal mass lesion or abscess
Ventricular system is normal in size and configuration
output.impression: No contraindication to lumbar puncture identified: no intracranial mass lesion, hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, or significant midline shift. Paranasal sinus disease with possible posterior ethmoid bone erosion raising concern for contiguous spread as source of meningitis. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Aerobic bottle #1 Streptococcus pneumoniae  (H), Aerobic bottle #2 Streptococcus pneumoniae  (H).

output.impression: Abnormal values: Right ear pure-tone average (PTA) 52 dB HL dB HL (H), Left ear pure-tone average (PTA) 35 dB HL dB HL (H), Right ear speech discrimination 68% % (H), Left ear speech discrimination 84% % (H).

output.query: bacterial meningitis empiric treatment guidelines adults 2024

results.title: Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: Tunkel AR, Hartman BJ, Kaplan SL, et al.
results.key_finding: Empiric therapy for adults 18-50: ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h. Add ampicillin if >50 years or immunocompromised (Listeria coverage). Adjunctive dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h for 4 days, started before or with first dose of antibiotics.

results.title: Dexamethasone in Adults with Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: de Gans J, van de Beek D.
results.key_finding: Adjunctive dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg q6h for 4 days) reduces mortality and unfavorable outcomes in adults with pneumococcal meningitis. Greatest benefit when given before or concurrent with first antibiotic dose.

results.title: Community-Acquired Bacterial Meningitis in Adults: An Update
results.authors: van de Beek D, Brouwer MC, Thwaites GE, Tunkel AR.
results.key_finding: S. pneumoniae remains the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults. Duration of therapy for pneumococcal meningitis is typically 10-14 days. Early complications include cerebral edema, hydrocephalus, and seizures.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend empiric therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults aged 18-50 with ceftriaxone (2g IV every 12 hours) plus vancomycin (15-20 mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours). Ampicillin should be added for patients over 50 or those who are immunocompromised to cover Listeria monocytogenes. Adjunctive dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days) should be administered before or concurrent with the first dose of antibiotics, as it significantly reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis. Once susceptibilities confirm ceftriaxone-susceptible S. pneumoniae, vancomycin can be discontinued. Standard treatment duration for pneumococcal meningitis is 10-14 days.

output.proposed: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days
interactions: Dexamethasone may increase blood glucose levels, requiring closer monitoring and possible insulin adjustment in this diabetic patient
Dexamethasone may reduce CSF penetration of vancomycin; monitor clinical response closely if vancomycin is being used
warnings: Monitor blood glucose every 4-6 hours given known diabetes and expected steroid-induced hyperglycemia
Dexamethasone should ideally be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics for maximal benefit; benefit is reduced if given after antibiotics have been started
Consider stress-dose steroids if patient develops adrenal insufficiency symptoms after dexamethasone discontinuation
alternatives: No alternative adjunctive therapy with equivalent evidence for bacterial meningitis

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcal meningitis)

differential.key_distinguishing: Would expect lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal or mildly elevated protein, normal glucose, negative Gram stain; procalcitonin typically <0.5 ng/mL
Temporal lobe involvement on MRI, seizures, personality changes; CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with positive HSV PCR; no neutrophilic predominance
Thunderclap headache onset (seconds to minutes), xanthochromia in CSF, elevated RBCs in CSF not clearing with sequential tubes; no fever or neutrophilic pleocytosis
Subacute course over weeks, basilar meningeal enhancement, lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis, very low glucose but typically less fulminant course; AFB smear and TB PCR positive
Typically immunocompromised host (HIV/AIDS), subacute to chronic course, positive India ink preparation or cryptococcal antigen; CSF shows lymphocytic predominance

optimal_actions.action: Rapid clinical assessment including vital signs, neurological examination with assessment for meningeal signs, and Glasgow Coma Scale scoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Fever 39.7°C, tachycardia, nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs, GCS 12

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat blood cultures (2 sets from separate sites) BEFORE starting antibiotics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Blood cultures drawn; will eventually grow Streptococcus pneumoniae

optimal_actions.action: Administer empiric IV antibiotics immediately: ceftriaxone 2g IV plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV, along with adjunctive dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV (given before or concurrent with antibiotics)
Obtain non-contrast CT head to rule out mass lesion or elevated ICP before lumbar puncture (given altered mental status)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No mass lesion or obstructive hydrocephalus; mild cerebral edema; paranasal sinus disease

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement, CSF cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, and meningitis PCR panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Turbid CSF, elevated opening pressure 32 cmH2O, WBC 3200 with 94% neutrophils, protein 348, glucose 14, Gram-positive diplococci on stain

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat laboratory studies: CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, CRP, procalcitonin, lactate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, hyponatremia (SIADH), elevated inflammatory markers, elevated lactate

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for complications (empyema, abscess, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, infarction)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement, DWI restriction in sulci, paranasal sinus disease, no complications

optimal_actions.action: Narrow antibiotic therapy once susceptibilities available: discontinue vancomycin if isolate is ceftriaxone-susceptible; continue ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h for 10-14 days total
Continue adjunctive dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days; monitor blood glucose closely given diabetes
Manage hyponatremia with fluid restriction and serial sodium monitoring (likely SIADH)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sodium trending toward normal with fluid restriction

optimal_actions.action: Consult ENT for evaluation of paranasal sinus disease as potential source of infection
Obtain audiometry prior to discharge to screen for sensorineural hearing loss (common complication of pneumococcal meningitis)
Admit to ICU for close neurological monitoring, seizure watch, and repeat neurological assessments

critical_actions: Obtain blood cultures before administering antibiotics
Administer empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone plus vancomycin) immediately without delay for lumbar puncture or imaging
Administer adjunctive dexamethasone before or concurrent with first dose of antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis (cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture)
Obtain CT head before LP given altered mental status to rule out mass lesion
Narrow antibiotics based on culture and susceptibility results
Screen for hearing loss as a complication of pneumococcal meningitis
contraindicated_actions: Delaying empiric antibiotics to wait for LP or imaging results
Performing LP without prior CT head in a patient with altered mental status and potential elevated ICP
Omitting adjunctive dexamethasone in suspected pneumococcal meningitis
Using dexamethasone after antibiotics have already been started for several hours (reduced benefit)
Administering antibiotics that do not achieve adequate CSF penetration (e.g., first-generation cephalosporins)
Fluid overloading a patient with SIADH-related hyponatremia
Discharging without audiometric screening given risk of sensorineural hearing loss
key_reasoning_points: Classic triad of fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status combined with photophobia and positive meningeal signs (Kernig, Brudzinski) is highly suggestive of acute bacterial meningitis
Empiric antibiotics must be given immediately; the door-to-antibiotic time is a critical quality metric and delays increase mortality
CT head before LP is indicated in this patient due to altered mental status (GCS 12); however, CT should not delay antibiotics
CSF findings of neutrophilic pleocytosis >1000, very low glucose (<20 mg/dL with ratio <0.4), high protein (>200 mg/dL), and positive Gram stain are diagnostic of bacterial meningitis
Gram-positive diplococci on CSF Gram stain are characteristic of Streptococcus pneumoniae, the most common cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults
Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis when given before or with the first antibiotic dose (de Gans trial, NEJM 2002)
Chronic sinusitis with recent exacerbation may represent the primary source of infection via contiguous spread, warranting ENT evaluation
Hyponatremia (sodium 131) in the setting of bacterial meningitis is most likely SIADH, a common complication requiring fluid restriction
Procalcitonin >2 ng/mL strongly favors bacterial over viral meningitis and can be useful when CSF results are equivocal
Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common long-term complication of pneumococcal meningitis, occurring in 14-30% of survivors

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, altered mental status, positive meningeal signs, and textbook CSF findings with gram-positive diplococci
clinical_pearls: The classic triad of fever, neck stiffness, and altered mental status is present in only 44% of bacterial meningitis cases, but at least two of four symptoms (headache, fever, neck stiffness, altered mental status) are present in 95%
Jolt accentuation of headache (worsening with rapid horizontal rotation of the head) has high sensitivity for meningitis and can be a useful bedside test
S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults, accounting for approximately 50% of cases
Do not delay antibiotics for any reason - every hour of delay in antibiotic administration increases mortality by approximately 3%
Adjunctive dexamethasone must be given before or with the first antibiotic dose; giving it hours later provides no benefit and may be harmful
